name: Acoustics and Wave Physics
description: Wave propagation, phonons, metamaterials, nonlinear acoustics

results.name: Wave Equation
results.structural_conclusion: Wave propagation in continuous media is fundamentally governed by second-order partial differential equations that relate spatial and temporal derivatives, establishing that waves are local phenomena obeying causality.
results.source_citation: d'Alembert, J. (1747). Recherches sur la courbe que forme une corde tendue

results.name: Helmholtz Equation
results.structural_conclusion: Time-harmonic wave fields decompose into spatial modes that are eigenfunctions of the Laplacian, revealing that wave behavior is fundamentally determined by the geometry of the propagation domain.
results.source_citation: Helmholtz, H. (1860). Theorie der Luftschwingungen in Röhren

results.name: Rayleigh's Principle of Reciprocity
results.structural_conclusion: Acoustic systems possess a fundamental symmetry where source and receiver can be interchanged without affecting the transfer function, reflecting time-reversal invariance of the wave equation.
results.source_citation: Rayleigh, J.W.S. (1873). Some general theorems relating to vibrations

results.name: Bloch's Theorem for Periodic Media
results.structural_conclusion: Wave propagation in periodic structures is characterized by band gaps and pass bands, revealing that periodicity imposes fundamental constraints on allowable wave frequencies and propagation modes.
results.source_citation: Bloch, F. (1928). Über die Quantenmechanik der Elektronen in Kristallgittern

results.name: Acoustic Radiation Pressure
results.structural_conclusion: Acoustic waves carry momentum and exert forces on obstacles, demonstrating that wave phenomena possess mechanical attributes beyond energy transport.
results.source_citation: Rayleigh, J.W.S. (1902). On the pressure of vibrations

results.name: Phonon Theory of Lattice Vibrations
results.structural_conclusion: Lattice vibrations quantize into discrete quasiparticles (phonons), revealing that acoustic waves in crystals have a fundamentally quantum mechanical character at microscopic scales.
results.source_citation: Born, M. & von Karman, T. (1912). Über Schwingungen in Raumgittern

results.name: Acoustic Metamaterials with Negative Index
results.structural_conclusion: Engineered subwavelength structures can produce effective material properties impossible in natural materials, demonstrating that wave behavior depends on structure at scales below the wavelength.
results.source_citation: Liu, Z. et al. (2000). Locally resonant sonic materials. Science 289, 1734

results.name: KdV Equation for Nonlinear Waves
results.structural_conclusion: Nonlinear wave propagation exhibits soliton solutions that maintain their shape through a precise balance of nonlinearity and dispersion, revealing integrability in certain nonlinear systems.
results.source_citation: Korteweg, D.J. & de Vries, G. (1895). On the change of form of long waves

results.name: Acoustic Cloaking
results.structural_conclusion: Coordinate transformations in the wave equation enable the design of materials that guide waves around objects, demonstrating that wave propagation paths can be arbitrarily controlled through material properties.
results.source_citation: Cummer, S.A. & Schurig, D. (2007). One path to acoustic cloaking. New J. Phys. 9, 45

results.name: Snell's Law and Critical Angle
results.structural_conclusion: Wave refraction at interfaces is governed by the continuity of phase along the boundary, establishing that material discontinuities impose geometric constraints on wave propagation.
results.source_citation: Snell, W. (1621). Unpublished manuscript, later in Descartes' Dioptrique

results.name: Acoustic Levitation
results.structural_conclusion: Standing acoustic waves create stable potential wells that can trap objects against gravity, demonstrating that wave interference patterns create effective force fields.
results.source_citation: Brandt, E.H. (1989). Levitation in physics. Science 243, 349

results.name: Time Reversal Acoustics
results.structural_conclusion: Wave propagation through complex media can be reversed to refocus at the source, revealing that scattering processes preserve information about the source despite apparent randomization.
results.source_citation: Fink, M. (1992). Time reversal of ultrasonic fields. IEEE Trans. Ultrason. Ferroelectr. 39, 555

results.name: Anderson Localization of Acoustic Waves
results.structural_conclusion: Disorder can completely halt wave propagation through destructive interference, demonstrating that randomness imposes fundamental limits on transport even in classical wave systems.
results.source_citation: Weaver, R.L. (1990). Anderson localization of ultrasound. Wave Motion 12, 129

results.name: Topological Acoustics
results.structural_conclusion: Acoustic systems can exhibit topologically protected edge states immune to backscattering, revealing that global geometric properties of band structures determine local wave propagation characteristics.
results.source_citation: Yang, Z. et al. (2015). Topological acoustics. Phys. Rev. Lett. 114, 114301

results.name: Exceptional Points in Non-Hermitian Acoustics
results.structural_conclusion: Non-Hermitian acoustic systems exhibit exceptional points where eigenvalues and eigenvectors coalesce, revealing that loss and gain create new topological structures in parameter space.
results.source_citation: Zhu, X. et al. (2014). PT-symmetric acoustics. Phys. Rev. X 4, 031042

structural_conclusion: Acoustics reveals that wave phenomena are fundamentally constrained by geometry, symmetry, and topology, with propagation determined by the interplay between local material properties and global structural features. The field demonstrates that waves can be controlled through engineered structures at subwavelength scales, while nonlinearity, disorder, and non-Hermiticity introduce qualitatively new behaviors including solitons, localization, and topological protection. These results establish that acoustic waves serve as a classical analog for quantum phenomena while possessing unique mechanical attributes that enable force generation and material manipulation.
